subject: Physics
title: The Shapiro Delay
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Shapiro Delay usable.

hook.text: Send a radar signal past the Sun and it arrives late. Gravity slows down time—even for light.

buildup.text: Irwin Shapiro predicted in 1964 that signals passing near a massive body would be delayed.

discovery.text: Bouncing radar off Venus when it passed behind the Sun confirmed the delay within 5% accuracy.

twist.text: Modern tests using the Cassini spacecraft have confirmed the Shapiro delay to 0.002% precision.

climax.text: This is the fourth classic test of general relativity—and one of the most precisely confirmed.

punchline.text: Light near the Sun runs late. Gravity slows even light.

quiz.question: What is the Shapiro delay?
options: Signals are delayed when passing near a massive object
Light speeds up near heavy objects
Radio waves change frequency near the Sun
